title: Cannae: Rome's Darkest Day
qa_notes: Scene count: 6; total runtime ~55s of narration audio (estimate).
Direct continuation of episode 10's hook (two years after the Trebia, Hannibal faces Rome's largest-ever army at Cannae).

Villain-strength check: Rome fields the largest army it has ever assembled — roughly twice Hannibal's numbers — drawing on the 'endless reserves' established in episode 10, making the coming encirclement feel impossible. Pass.

scenes.title: Cold Open
scenes.description: Two years after the Trebia, Rome has had enough of losing. It musters the single largest army it has ever assembled — roughly eighty-six thousand men, nearly double Hannibal's force — and marches to a small town called Cannae to end the war in one battle.
scenes.narration: Two years after the Trebia, Rome had had enough. It mustered the largest army it had ever assembled in its history — roughly eighty-six thousand men, nearly double the size of Hannibal's force — and marched to a small town called Cannae, determined to end the war in a single, overwhelming battle.
scenes.video_prompt: Cinematic historical documentary style, Empire Decoded. Vast aerial shot of an enormous Roman army — tens of thousands of legionaries in tight formation, banners and eagles raised — marching across the plains of southern Italy toward a small town, dust clouds rising for miles. In the distance, a much smaller Carthaginian force watches from a low ridge. Photoreal detail, dramatic morning lighting, accurate period costumes and armor, film-grade composition, 16:9.

scenes.title: Setting the Stage
scenes.description: The Roman consuls, commanding alternating days, plan a simple, brutal strategy: mass the legions into one enormous, deep formation and use overwhelming numbers to smash straight through Hannibal's center. With nearly two-to-one odds, it seems unstoppable.
scenes.narration: The Roman consuls had a simple plan: pack the legions into one enormous, unusually deep formation and use sheer mass to smash straight through Hannibal's center. With nearly two soldiers for every one of Hannibal's, it must have seemed unstoppable — a wall of Roman steel that nothing could hold back.
scenes.video_prompt: Cinematic historical documentary style, Empire Decoded. Wide shot of the Roman army forming up into an enormous, densely packed rectangular formation, ranks stretching back farther than the eye can see, officers on horseback giving final orders. Across the field, Hannibal's noticeably smaller army forms a thin curved line. Photoreal detail, dramatic lighting, accurate period costumes and architecture, film-grade composition, 16:9.

scenes.title: Rising Tension
scenes.description: Hannibal makes a strange choice: he arranges his weakest troops in a thin, bulging crescent at the center, with his best veteran infantry held back on the flanks. As the battle begins, the Roman mass pushes the center back exactly as Hannibal intended — drawing them deeper into a trap.
scenes.narration: Hannibal made a choice that looked like madness. He put his weakest troops in a thin, bulging line at the center — and held his best veteran infantry back on the flanks. When the battle began, the massive Roman formation did exactly what gravity demanded: it pushed the thin center back, and back, and back. The Romans thought they were winning. They were walking into a bag.
scenes.video_prompt: Cinematic historical documentary style, Empire Decoded. Wide battlefield shot, the center of Hannibal's line bowing inward under pressure from the massive Roman formation pushing forward, while on both flanks, Hannibal's veteran infantry and cavalry remain in tense, disciplined formation, watching and waiting. Photoreal detail, intense midday lighting, film-grade composition, 16:9.

scenes.title: The Clash
scenes.description: As the Roman center bulges deep into Hannibal's line, his veteran flanks wheel inward and his cavalry — having already destroyed Rome's much smaller cavalry wings — slams into the Roman rear. The largest army Rome has ever fielded is completely surrounded.
scenes.narration: As the Roman center pushed deep into what looked like a collapsing line, Hannibal's veteran flanks wheeled inward like a closing door. At the same moment, his cavalry — having already crushed Rome's far smaller cavalry wings — slammed into the Roman rear. In minutes, the largest army Rome had ever fielded was completely surrounded, packed so tightly that many soldiers couldn't even raise their weapons.
scenes.video_prompt: Cinematic historical documentary style, Empire Decoded. Dramatic aerial-style battle shot, the massive Roman formation now completely encircled, Carthaginian infantry closing in from both flanks and cavalry crashing into the rear, the dense Roman ranks compressed so tightly that chaos and panic ripple through them. Dust, blood-red light, overwhelming scale. Photoreal detail, dramatic lighting, film-grade composition, 16:9.

scenes.title: Turning Point
scenes.description: What follows is not a battle but a slaughter. By the end of the day, as many as fifty thousand Roman soldiers are dead — more than were lost by any army, on any single day, in the history of organized warfare up to that point. Rome's military leadership is decimated.
scenes.narration: What followed wasn't a battle anymore — it was a slaughter. By the end of the day, as many as fifty thousand Roman soldiers lay dead — more men killed in a single day than any army in history up to that point. Among the dead were dozens of senators and most of Rome's senior military leadership, wiped out in an afternoon.
scenes.video_prompt: Cinematic historical documentary style, Empire Decoded. Somber, wide aerial shot of the Cannae battlefield at dusk, an almost incomprehensible field of fallen soldiers stretching to the horizon, smoke and dust settling, a few exhausted survivors wandering among the dead. Photoreal detail, somber red-orange sunset lighting, film-grade composition, 16:9.

scenes.title: Legacy
scenes.description: Cannae remains studied in military academies to this day as the ultimate example of tactical encirclement. And yet, against all odds, Rome refuses to surrender — rebuilding its armies yet again, setting up a war of attrition that will eventually turn against Carthage itself.
scenes.narration: Cannae is still studied in military academies today as the textbook example of total encirclement. And yet, against every expectation, Rome refused to surrender. It rebuilt its shattered armies once again — and began a long war of attrition that, years later, would turn the tables on Carthage for good. Subscribe to Empire Decoded for the next chapter.
scenes.video_prompt: Cinematic historical documentary style, Empire Decoded. Final shot, the Roman Senate at night, grim-faced senators in mourning togas standing firm despite the devastating news, candles burning. Transition to a map overlay showing new Roman legions being raised across Italy once more, small but determined arrows pointing back toward Carthage. Photoreal detail, somber but resolute tone, film-grade composition, 16:9.

music.prompt: Epic historical documentary score, cinematic orchestral, large-scale battle and tragedy theme. Begins with a confident, marching theme representing Rome's massive army, shifts into a tense, tightening theme with descending strings as the trap closes, peaks with an overwhelming, dissonant, percussive climax representing total encirclement, and resolves into a slow, mournful string elegy for the aftermath, ending on a single low resolute note representing Rome's refusal to quit. Instrumental only, optional wordless choir for the elegy. Style reference: Hans Zimmer historical trailer score.
